---
title: ""
page-layout: full
toc: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="page-header container">
  <p class="page-header-label">Education</p>
  <h1 class="page-header-title">Teaching</h1>
  <p class="page-header-sub" style="font-style:italic;">
    Numbers do not speak for themselves. Someone has to understand the
    audience and communicate so they listen and act.
  </p>
  <p style="color:#6b7280; font-size:0.9rem; margin-top:0.75rem;">
    More in <a href="https://www.ncsc.org/sites/default/files/media/document/NCSC-Trends-2025.pdf"><em>Telling
    Your Court's Data Story</em></a>, Trends in State Courts, 2025. Presented
    at the National Association for Court Management.
  </p>
</div>
```

## How I Teach {.section-heading}

Give the room a number worth arguing about, then let the argument do the
teaching. The debate starts with a rap score and ends with people questioning
their own metrics.

The six ideas I come back to most often:

::: {.concept-list}
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#f3edf3; border-color:#8C6A8D; color:#6b4f6c;">Construct validity</span>
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#fdf0ec; border-color:#E98973; color:#c96b52;">Sampling and bias</span>
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#f8f5f0; border-color:#D2B1A3; color:#2B2D42;">Multidimensional measurement</span>
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#f3edf3; border-color:#6b4f6c; color:#6b4f6c;">Uncertainty communication</span>
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#fdf0ec; border-color:#c96b52; color:#c96b52;">Visualization design</span>
<span class="concept-pill" style="background:#F8F7F4; border-color:#888; color:#333;">Narrative framing</span>
:::

---

## Teaching Materials {.section-heading}

Every one below is live and self-contained. Open it and try it: each is built to
start an argument, not settle one.

```{=html}
<div class="project-grid container">

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Data Storytelling · Teaching Module</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">The Hip-Hop Periodic Table</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      A 90-minute session built on a dataset of 173 hip-hop acts, each scored on
      five parts of lyricism. It opens with a quick gut rating of a favorite
      artist, then spends the next 45 minutes on the real question: what did you
      just measure? By the end the room is debating construct validity using its
      own performance measures.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#8C6A8D;"></span>R / Quarto</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#E98973;"></span>Interactive</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#D2B1A3;"></span>Full session</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../hiphop/hiphop_periodic_table.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#8C6A8D;">
      Explore the periodic table →
    </a>
    <p style="font-size:0.85rem; margin-top:0.5rem; color:#6b7280;">
      Companion materials:
      <a href="../hiphop/hiphop_dashboard.html">analytics dashboard</a> ·
      <a href="../hiphop/data_dictionary.html">data dictionary</a>
    </p>
  </div>

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Measurement · Cold Open</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">The Barnum Test</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      A short exercise that builds a personalized profile, asks how accurate it
      feels, then reveals that everyone got the same profile and the ratings were
      never used at all. The point is not that people were fooled. It is that a
      measure everyone scores high on has not measured anyone. Works as a cold
      open before the longer session, or on its own.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#8C6A8D;"></span>HTML / JS</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#E98973;"></span>Interactive</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#D2B1A3;"></span>Construct validity</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../barnum/barnum_test.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#8C6A8D;">
      Take the Barnum Test →
    </a>
  </div>

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Measurement · Aging</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">How Old Is Old?</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      Set a slider to the age at which someone becomes "old," "a senior," "too old
      to drive," or "too old to start over." The answers show how little we agree,
      and how the law pins hard numbers (18, 50, 65) onto a boundary nature never
      drew. A study in turning a fuzzy idea into an exact number.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#8C6A8D;"></span>HTML / JS</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#E98973;"></span>Interactive</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#D2B1A3;"></span>Short exercise</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../howold/how_old_is_old.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#8C6A8D;">
      Try How Old Is Old? →
    </a>
  </div>

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Prediction · Risk</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">Trust Your Gut?</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      Review realistic case files, predict who returns to court, then see your
      accuracy scored against a simple two-variable rule that beats nearly
      everyone. A lesson in why expert intuition keeps losing to a checklist.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#8C6A8D;"></span>HTML / JS</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#E98973;"></span>Interactive</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#D2B1A3;"></span>Short exercise</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../gut/trust_your_gut.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#8C6A8D;">
      Trust your gut? →
    </a>
  </div>

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Evaluation Practice · Decision Tool</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">Which Evaluation Should We Do?</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      Say what you have and what you want to know, and every evaluation approach
      lands in one of three piles: what fits, what is the wrong tool and why, and
      what is close, with the gap named and the work to close it spelled out.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#8C6A8D;"></span>R / Quarto</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#6b4f6c;"></span>Decision tool</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#E98973;"></span>Interactive</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../evalpicker/eval_picker.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#E98973;">
      Open the evaluation picker →
    </a>
  </div>

  <div class="project-card">
    <span class="project-card-tag">Data Storytelling · Decision Tool</span>
    <p class="project-card-title">Which Format Fits Best?</p>
    <p class="project-card-desc">
      Say who you are trying to reach and what you want the work to do, and every
      delivery format sorts into three piles: the ones that fit, the ones that
      could work with a tweak, and the ones that are the wrong tool here and why.
    </p>
    <div class="project-card-meta">
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#4F6B33;"></span>R / Quarto</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#B0791C;"></span>Decision tool</span>
      <span><span class="project-card-accent" style="background:#BC4634;"></span>Interactive</span>
    </div>
    <br>
    <a href="../formatpicker/format_picker.html" style="font-weight:600; color:#4F6B33;">
      Open the format picker →
    </a>
  </div>

</div>
```

::: {.callout-tip title="Session Design Note"}
The Hip-Hop session opens with a quick exercise: rate a favorite artist's
lyricism on a scale of 1 to 10, right away, no second-guessing. Then comes the
real question. What did you just measure? For the Barnum Test, ask for the
accuracy ratings out loud before the reveal. People commit to a number in front
of the room, and the reveal lands harder than it does when they rate it
privately.
:::

---

## The Usual Subjects {.section-heading}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Measurement and metrics**

- Building performance measures that hold up
- When composite scores help and when they mislead
- Communicating confidence and uncertainty to non-technical stakeholders
- The politics of how organizational metrics get designed
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Data visualization**

- Choosing chart types that match your argument
- Designing for uncertainty, not false precision
- Interactive vs. static, and when to use each
- Common visualization mistakes in stakeholder reporting
:::
::::
